content.problemStatement:
A small store wants a simple inventory system to manage its products. Your task is to build this using **Object-Oriented Programming** principles.

Requirements

Create a `Product` class with:



Fields: `id`, `name`, `quantity`
A method `display()` to print product details



In the main program, support the following operations:



`add id name quantity` → Add a new product
`sell id quantity` → Reduce quantity of the product
`restock id quantity` → Increase quantity
`display` → Display all current products in the format:

`ID: x, Name: y, Quantity: z`



Input Format

First line: An integer `N`, the number of operations
Next `N` lines: Each line is one of the supported operations


Output Format

For every `display` command, show all products in the order they were added.
constraints: name consists of alphabetic characters without spaces and length <= 20
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of operations. The next N lines each contain one operation in one of the following forms:
add id name quantity
sell id quantity
restock id quantity
display
content.outputFormat: For every display command of every test case, output the current list of products in the order they were added. Each product is printed on its own line in the format: ID: x, Name: y, Quantity: z. Separate the outputs of different test cases with a newline (i.e., after finishing a test case, print a blank line if there is another test case).

sampleTestCases.output: ID: 1, Name: Apple, Quantity: 7\nID: 2, Name: Banana, Quantity: 15
sampleTestCases.explanation: Two products are added. Apple quantity becomes 10-3=7 after selling 3. Banana is restocked to 5+10=15. The display command prints both products in insertion order. The final sell operation tries to sell 20 Apples but only 7 are available, so nothing changes.

sampleTestCases.output: ID: 1, Name: Orange, Quantity: 30\nID: 2, Name: Grape, Quantity: 15
sampleTestCases.explanation: Orange is added with 20, then restocked by 10 → 30. Grape stays at 15. Display prints both in the order they were added.
Pear starts with 5, 3 are sold, leaving 2. Display shows the single product.

solution.approach:
For each test case we maintain two structures: (1) a vector/ArrayList/list that stores Product objects in the order they are added, and (2) a hash map (unordered_map, HashMap, dict) that maps a product id to its position in the vector.

When an add operation arrives we create a new Product, push it to the vector and store its index in the map.
When sell or restock arrives we locate the product via the map (O(1) average) and adjust its quantity, ensuring that we never reduce below zero (the statement says to ignore the operation if not enough stock).
When display arrives we simply iterate over the vector and print each product using the required format.

All operations are processed sequentially, giving an overall O(N) time per test case and O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of operations. Each operation is O(1) except display which is O(current number of products).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(P) per test case, where P is the number of distinct products added (P ≤ N).
